title: The 'it' Keyword
description: A deep dive into Kotlin's magic variable.

slides.title: The Implicit Parameter 🤫
slides.content:
## The Hidden 'it'

We briefly touched on `it` during the Collections chapter when using `.filter { }`. Now that you know that `.filter { }` is just a Higher-Order Function accepting a Trailing Lambda, let's understand how `it` really works.

slides.title: Implicit Naming 🪄
slides.content:
If a Lambda only accepts exactly **ONE single parameter**, Kotlin considers explicitly typing out `value ->` to be tedious boilerplate.

It automatically provides a hidden variable named `it` that perfectly points to that one incoming parameter.

quizData.question: If you define a Lambda that accepts TWO parameters `(Int, String)`, can you just use `return it`?

options.text: Yes, 'it' points to the first parameter.
No, because 'it' only automatically generates if there is EXACTLY ONE singular parameter.

quizData.explanation: If there is ambiguity (2 or more parameters), Kotlin refuses to generate `it`. You must explicitly name them yourself (e.g., `{ age, name -> ... }`).

slides.title: Naming Over 'it' 📛
slides.content:
## When NOT to use 'it'

While `it` is amazing for tiny, 1-line lambdas (like a `.filter { it > 5 }`), it can become very confusing in massive, multi-line blocks of logic. 
If you forget what `it` represents down on line 45, it is always safer to explicitly name your parameter instead of relying on the magic `it`.
